messages.content: You must return JSON only. No markdown, no code fences, no commentary. Return a single JSON object with keys: mode, topic, opinion, reasoning_summary.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.","persona":{"age":43,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"mechanic","city_of_living":"Washington DC","employment_status":"employed part-time","education_level":"lower secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"vocational diploma","parent_2":"vocational diploma"},"current_marital_status":"divorced","children":2,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"curious","religious_beliefs":"Agnostic","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend several hours daily on social media","hobbies":["board games","travel","cycling"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":77,"level":"high"},"conscientiousness":{"score":8,"level":"low"},"extraversion":{"score":29,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":20,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":57,"level":"moderate"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

  "opinion": "As a 43-year-old mechanic living in Washington DC, I've had to navigate discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines in a way that fits my hands-on, practical mindset. With two kids and a part-time job, I don't have time for endless debates, but my curiosity keeps me digging into things. I spend hours on social media daily, so I see all sorts of opinions, from wild conspiracy theories to solid medical advice. My approach is to focus on what works, like how I fix cars—look at the evidence, test things out, and avoid getting bogged down in drama. For me, managing these talks means staying grounded in facts from reliable sources, like the CDC or local health departments, because I've learned that not everything online holds up under scrutiny. I'm agnostic, so I don't let religious beliefs cloud my judgment, and my high openness score means I'm willing to consider new info, but my low conscientiousness keeps me from over-planning—I just adapt as I go. In conversations, I keep it straightforward, sharing my own experiences, like how getting vaccinated helped me stay healthy for work and my kids' activities. I avoid heated arguments because my low extraversion and agreeableness make me prefer quiet, one-on-one chats over big confrontations. Ultimately, I believe in personal choice but stress the importance of community health, drawing on my travels and hobbies to see how different places handle things. It's about balancing curiosity with practicality, without letting anxiety from my moderate neuroticism take over.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a 43-year-old mechanic living in Washington DC, I've had to navigate discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines in a way that fits my hands-on, practical mindset. With two kids and a part-time job, I don't have time for endless debates, but my curiosity keeps me digging into things. I spend hours on social media daily, so I see all sorts of opinions, from wild conspiracy theories to solid medical advice. My approach is to focus on what works, like how I fix cars—look at the evidence, test things out, and avoid getting bogged down in drama. For me, managing these talks means staying grounded in facts from reliable sources, like the CDC or local health departments, because I've learned that not everything online holds up under scrutiny. I'm agnostic, so I don't let religious beliefs cloud my judgment, and my high openness score means I'm willing to consider new info, but my low conscientiousness keeps me from over-planning—I just adapt as I go. In conversations, I keep it straightforward, sharing my own experiences, like how getting vaccinated helped me stay healthy for work and my kids' activities. I avoid heated arguments because my low extraversion and agreeableness make me prefer quiet, one-on-one chats over big confrontations. Ultimately, I believe in personal choice but stress the importance of community health, drawing on my travels and hobbies to see how different places handle things. It's about balancing curiosity with practicality, without letting anxiety from my moderate neuroticism take over.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion by integrating persona traits: age (43) suggests life experience; occupation (mechanic) implies practical, hands-on approach; city (Washington DC) adds urban context; employment (part-time) and children (2) indicate busy lifestyle; education (lower secondary) and parents' vocational backgrounds shape no-nonsense attitude; migration (native-born) provides American perspective; psychological feature (curious) drives information-seeking; religious beliefs (Agnostic) avoid bias; time on social media (several hours daily) informs exposure to diverse views; hobbies (board games, travel, cycling) suggest strategic and experiential thinking; OCEAN traits: high openness (score 77) fosters adaptability and interest in new ideas, low conscientiousness (score 8) leads to flexible, non-rigid planning, low extraversion (score 29) prefers low-key interactions, low agreeableness (score 20) avoids people-pleasing, moderate neuroticism (score 57) manages anxiety pragmatically. Tone is direct, informal, and grounded, avoiding academic jargon, with examples tied to personal life (e.g., work, kids, social media).
response_parsed.tone: direct, informal, practical, and grounded, with a slight edge from low agreeableness, avoiding flowery language.
